text: Local recurrence is one of the most important problems of rectal cancer surgery. One of the ways to reduce frequency of relapses is addition of lateral pelvic dissection to traditional TME. While TME is currently the standard of treatment for rectal cancer, lateral pelvic dissection is a debatable option and is performed in various clinical scenarios.

The first is implementation of lateral pelvic dissection to virtually all patients. This approach is generally accepted in Japan, where lateral dissection is recommended for all rectal tumors located below the peritoneum of the pelvic floor or invading muscularis propria.

The second approach is wide use of neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy before performing TME. which allowed many European centers to reduce local recurrence dramatically. In our center, we use chemoradiotherapy routinely for locally advanced lower rectal cancer and in cases of remaining metastatic lateral lymph nodes on MRI after the end of neoadjuvant treatment. We perform lateral lymph node dissection on the affected side simultaneously to TME.

The video displays the pelvic anatomy in lateral projection, highlighting the pelvic bones, greater and lesser sciatic notches, and the obturator foramen. Pelvic ligaments, including the sacro-tubercular and sacro-spinal ligaments, are shown, illustrating their role in converting the sciatic notches into foramens. The inguinal ligament is also visible.

The video displays muscular structures within the pelvis, including the psoas major muscle extending from the pelvis to the thigh, the piriformis muscle originating from the sacrum and passing through the greater sciatic foramen, and the internal obturator muscle starting from the obturator membrane and exiting through the lesser sciatic foramen. Additionally, the lumbosacral trunk is shown, originating from sacral nerves and forming the sciatic nerve, the largest nerve bundle in the pelvis.

The obturator nerve is visible emerging from beneath the psoas muscle, heading towards the obturator canal. The common iliac vessels are seen dividing into external and internal branches upon entering the pelvis. The external iliac artery is observed extending towards the thigh, while the internal iliac artery splits into anterior and posterior parts. The large superior gluteal artery is seen emerging from the posterior division, passing through the greater sciatic foramen. Additionally, the lateral sacral artery and the umbilical artery, originating from the anterior division of the internal iliac artery, are visible.

The obturator artery is visualized entering the obturator canal. In female patients, the uterine artery is observed. The inferior gluteal and pudendal arteries exit the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen. The ureter crosses the external iliac artery on the right side as it enters the pelvis, descending and being crossed by the uterine artery posteriorly. The bladder and uterus are also visible in the surgical field.

The laparoscopic view shows the bladder, cervix, and uterus with appendages. The uterine artery is visible in the paracervix, separating the paravesical and parorectal spaces, with the latter known as Lutzko's space.

The surgical team proceeds to the lateral pelvic lymphadenectomy stage in a female pelvis. The assistant applies traction to the round ligament of the uterus, revealing anatomical structures including the spaces of Ogabayashi and Ibuki medial to the ureter.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum parallel to the psoas muscle along the upper edge of the external iliac vessels. The posterior and anterior leaves of the broad ligament of the uterus are sequentially peeled off the lateral pelvic wall, revealing the umbilical artery as an anatomical landmark. Wide axes are created near large vessels before proceeding to dissection, and the obturator nerve is visualized.

The surgeon expands the dissection area anteriorly, revealing the external iliac vessels bending over the pubic bone under the inguinal ligament. The anterior border of dissection, the pubic bone, is visible. The surgeon identifies all main landmarks of the paravesical space and proceeds to create the parorectal space. The dorsal border of dissection is determined by the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels. The peritoneum incision is expanded to visualize the external iliac artery and vein. The left ureter is seen crossing the iliac vessels at their bifurcation, marking the dorsal dissection boundary.

The surgeon expands the dissection space, visualizing the edge of the large lumbar muscle, marking the upper limit of the dissection zone. The ureter is seen medially, while the uterine artery at the border of the parametri and paracervix creates the parorectal and paravesical spaces. The paravesical space is located anterior to the uterine artery, with the parametri and paracervix forming its dorsal wall and the pubic bone, marked in gray, forming the anterior wall.

The surgical field shows the paravesical space, marked in green, divided by the umbilical artery into lateral and medial parts. The pararectal space, located behind the uterine artery and laterally to the ureter, is visible and named after Lutsko. Medially to the ureter, the projection line for the creation of Okabayashi and Yobuki spaces is observed. Splunking nerves, including the hypogastric nerve and the lower hypogastric plexus, are seen passing through these interfacial spaces.
The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy by dissecting along the edge of the large lumbar muscle towards the anterior border, moving the layer of fatty tissue medially while applying traction.

The surgeon separates the fat from the front angle of the paravesical space, exposing the wall of the distal part of the external iliac vessels. Fatty tissue is carefully peeled off the pelvic wall to preserve the vessels. The cupal ligament is identified as the front dissection boundary. The surgeon continues dissection in a caudal direction, peeling fatty tissue from the large lumbar muscle. The lower border of dissection is marked by the lower edge of the obturator nerve and the level of the gluteal veins. The internal obturator muscle and the lateral wall of the paravesical space are visualized, along with the obturator nerve.

The surgeon opens the nerve and cuts the fiber along its edge, facilitating the subsequent stages of lymphadenectomy. The fatty tissue is removed starting from the external group of lymph nodes, exposing the iliac vessels. The internal group of lymph nodes is then removed along with the fatty tissue from the iliac vessels. The obturator group has already been mobilized, and the projection of the obturator neurovascular bundle is visible and intact. The lymph nodes are removed, marking the completion of the dissection and the final stage of the operation. The final view of the surgical field is presented.
